blocks.content:
## Eval Systems & Quality Gates

*How to know the AI got better or worse before users find out*

Master execution-based evaluation, visual regression, regression detection, LLM-as-judge with human calibration, eval infrastructure as a product, drift detection, and pre-deployment gates.

## Modules in This Level

### 5.1 Why Code-Gen Evals Are Uniquely Hard

Evaluation is the discipline that separates AI products that improve reliably from those that drift unpredictably. For a Platform PM owning Make, understanding why code-gen evals are structurally diff

### 5.2 Execution-Based Evaluation

Execution-based evaluation is the only reliable way to determine if AI-generated code actually functions. Static analysis catches some problems (syntax errors, type errors), but a surprising amount of

### 5.3 Visual Regression Testing

Visual regression testing is one of the most underinvested areas in AI code generation product infrastructure — and one of the areas where Make has a structural advantage over competitors. Because Mak

### 5.4 Regression Detection Across Releases

Regression detection across releases is the discipline of ensuring that improvements in one area of a system do not silently degrade other areas. For an AI code generation platform, this is particular

### 5.5 LLM-as-Judge and Human-in-the-Loop

LLM-as-judge is now a standard technique in AI evaluation, but using it well requires understanding its failure modes, the critical importance of rubric design, and how to calibrate it against human j

### 5.6 Eval Infrastructure as a Product

The eval system is infrastructure — and like all infrastructure, it has users with different needs. Treating the eval system as a product, with its own roadmap and user requirements, is the difference

### 5.7 The Eval-Driven Development Loop

Eval data is a compass, not just a report card. The most valuable use of a mature eval system is not confirming that quality is maintained — it is revealing where to invest next. Understanding how to
